query: Reseach how to deal with modifications vs indistingushablity when it comes to integrals like Ito Integral, Stiljes Integral, Fractional Ornstein-Ulhenbeck Integral and how do they all connect to RFSV. If it doesnt exist, invent a therem or find a way to connect it.

selected.title: Volterra roughness scaling law
selected.statement: Assume log-volatility X is generated by a Volterra kernel K with local singularity K(t,s) ~ c (t-s)^{H-1/2} for H in (0,1/2). Then the q-variation and increment variance of X at fine scales obey a power law whose slope identifies H, and realized short-horizon volatility inherits the same roughness exponent up to lower-order microstructure distortion.
assumptions: X admits a Volterra representation with kernel singularity exponent H-1/2.
Observed prices admit a sufficiently fine daily or intraday volatility proxy.
Microstructure noise is lower order on the estimation scale or explicitly corrected.
Empirical scope anchored to SPY US Equity.

variables.RV_{t,Δ}: realized variance proxy over scale Δ

selected.motivation: Rough-volatility books usually encode the kernel singularity analytically, while market data encodes the same object empirically through variance scaling. QuantAI should bridge those two views directly.
empirical_signature: compare full-sample hurst-style estimate to rolling realized-volatility roughness estimates.
symbolic_agenda: Derive increment covariance from the Volterra representation under kernel asymptotics.
Show how variance scaling transfers from X to a realized-volatility proxy under discretization.
failure_conditions: Estimated slope is not stable across scales.
Jump contamination dominates the inferred roughness exponent.
Kernel singularity cannot be identified from the available observation grid.
Add explicit symbolic tasks with lhs/rhs identities or qualitative constraints.

Indeed, any naive attempt to put it in Stratonovich form, Z t Z t “ f(bWs) ◦dWs := f( bWs)dWs + (Itô–Stratonovich correction),” (10.10) 0 0 or, in the spirit of Wong–Zakai approximations, Z t Z t “ s )dWεs ,” (10.11) 0 f(bWs) ◦dWs := ε→0lim 0 f( bWε must fail for H < 12. The Itô–Stratonovich correction is given by the quadratic covariation, defined (whenever possible) as the limit, in probability, of X ( f( bWv) −f( bWu))(Wv −Wu), (10.12) [u,v]∈π along any sequence (πn) of partitions with mesh-size tending to zero. Disregarding trivial sit- uations we see that this limit does not exist. For instance, when f(x) = x, fractional scaling immediately gives divergence (at rate H −12) of the expression (10.12). This issue also arises in the context of option pricing (Theorem 10.2 and Section 10.5). All theses problems remain present, of course, for the more complicated situation of “nonsimple” rough volatility, as dis- cussed in Section 10.4.

. The limiting model (Π, Γ) Let W be a Brownian motion on R+ := [0, ∞), and extend it to all of R by requiring Wx = 0 for x ≤0. We will frequently use the notation Z t Z t f(t)dWt, f(t) ⋄dWt (10.27) 0 0 which denote the Itô integral and the Skorokhod integral (which boils down to an Itô integral whenever the integrand is adapted), respectively. For background on Skorokhod integration we refer the reader to [216, Section 7.3]; Nualart’s ICM lecture [251] is also highly recommended. Skorokhod integrals have the distinct advantage of avoiding the need for an adapted integrand, but they coincide with Itô integrals once the integrand is adapted. For a reader unfamiliar with the (beautiful) theory of Skorokhod integration, it should be sufficient to simply think of an Itô integral with possibly nonadapted integrands for the purposes of this chapter. Whenever we make use of specific properties of Skorokhod integration we will make this explicit.

. Note that the expression R 0∞ φ(t) (bWt−bWs)m⋄dWt ill-defined as an Itô integral: Since we allow φ to have support below s, the domain of integration involves t with t < s, in which case ( bW(t) −bW(s))m is not adapted with respect to the filtration of W at time t. The concept of a Skorokhod integral is, in such cases, a natural extension of Itô’s notion of integration that boils down to the classical Itô integral once the integrand is adapted. The theory of rough paths morally corresponds to the choice φ(t) = 1[s,t′](t) for s < t′ (see also [147, Section 13.3.2]), which explains why issues of this kind never arise in the rough path framework. as an Itô integral: There is in fact a way to write the integral R 0∞ φ(t) (bWt −bWs)m ⋄dWt Expand ( bWt −bWs)m via the binomial theorem and “pull out” all factors depending on s (such a point of view is essentially behind Chen’s relation in (10.30)). However, as this seems like a rather nebulous definition, we consider it more convenient to work with a more appropriate notion of integration in this chapter.

trials.candidate_statement: Whenever a centered Gaussian process admits a deterministic kernel representation X_t = ∫_0^t K(t,s) dW_s, its exact covariance is determined by the bilinear form E[X_t X_u] = ∫_0^{t∧u} K(t,s)K(u,s) ds; for singular fractional kernels this identity should be treated as the primary object, with pathwise or fractional integral representations derived from it rather than imposed first.

trials.candidate_statement: If latent log-volatility follows a fractional Ornstein-Uhlenbeck dynamic with mean reversion λ and roughness H < 1/2, then short-lag autocovariance of log-volatility proxies decays hyperbolically at the very short horizon before crossing into an exponential mean-reversion regime at longer horizons.

. In fact, this theory would require W and bW to be independent (↔here: fully correlated), the resulting lift to be of geometric (i.e., Stratonovich) type ( ↔here: Itô integral), the Hurst parameter H > 1/4 ( ↔here: any H > 0), and an enhancement of at most 3 levels (level-3 rough paths) ( ↔here: ∼1/H levels). A third attempt, in view of the nongeometric nature of Itô integration, to use branched rough paths is also doomed, for it requires—like classical geometric rough path theory—all iterated integrals. In our case, there is already an obstacle at level 2, before the appearance of any branching, in that the full set of second iterated integrals R WdW, R Wd bW = ∗ ? . 10.0.2 Complications with rough volatility Due to loss of Markovianity, PDE methods are not applicable and neither are (off-the-shelf) Freidlin–Wentzell large deviation estimates (but see [138]). Moreover, the variance process in rough volatility models is not a semimartingale, which complicates, the use of several estab- lished stochastic analysis tools. In particular, rough volatility admits no Stratonovich formula- tion. Closely related is the fact that, one lacks a (Wong–Zakai type of) approximation theory for rough volatility. To see this, focus on the “simple” situation, that is (10.1), (10.3), so that Z · S t/S 0 = E f bWs dBs (t) . retrieved_sources.text: Bibliographic Notes 93 1940, but was killed later that year in action in the Second World War, andthus his results were lost until recently [26, 27] . Doob stressed the martingalenature of the Ito integral in his book [55] and proposed a general martingale integral. Doob's proposed development depended on a decomposition theo­ III)rem (the Doob-Meyer decomposition, Theorem 8 of Chap. which did not yet exist. Meyer proved this decomposition theorem in [163, 164], and com­ mented that a theory of stochastic integration was now possible. This wasbegun by Courrege [36], and extended by Kunita and Watanabe [134], whorevealed an elegant structure of square integrable martingales and established a general change of variables formula. Meyer [166, 167, 168, 169] extendedKunita and Watanabe's work, realizing that the restriction of integrands to predictable processes is essential. He also extended the integrals to local mar­ tingales, which had been introduced earlier by Ito and Watanabe [102]

Chapter 2 Pricing under rough volatility24 Christian Bayer, Peter K. Friz, Jim Gatheral 2.1 Introduction From an analysis of the time series of realized variance using recent high frequency data, Gatheral, Jaisson, and Rosenbaum [166] showed that the logarithm of realized variance behaves essentially as a fractional Brownian motion (fBm) with Hurst exponent H of order 0.1, at any reasonable time scale. The following stationary rough fractional stochastic volatility (RFSV) model was proposed: dS t = µtdt + VtdZt, S t Vt = exp{Xt}, t ∈[0, T], (2.1) where µt is a suitable drift term, Zt is a standard Brownian motion, Xt is a fractional Ornstein– Uhlenbeck process (fOU process for short) satisfying dXt = νdWHt −α(Xt −m)dt, where m ∈R, and ν and α are positive parameters (see [92]), with Z and WH correlated in general
